chunks.text: Philosophy begins with doubting what we take for granted.
" What is justice?
" " What is a good life?
" " What can we know?
" These questions have been asked from ancient Greece, 2500 years ago
to the present day.
Plato argued that beyond the visible world there lay a perfect world of " Ideas.
" Aristotle thought that we could approach truth only through concrete experience.
The contrast between the two became the archetype of the history of philosophy.
In the seventeenth century, Descartes wrote his famous words, " I think, therefore I am.
" Even if one doubts everything
the existence of the self that doubts cannot be doubted.
This became the starting point of modern philosophy
and the problem of consciousness was placed at the center.
Kant asked what a moral act truly was.
He said
" Ask yourself whether the principle of your action could become a principle for all.
" He tried to set the standard of good and evil in an absolute way.
Nietzsche doubted the very system of existing moral values.
His declaration " God is dead " was a radical question thrown at a modern society whose traditional values had collapsed.
In philosophy, the act of continuing to ask has more meaning than finding an answer.
To face an abstract question is the most fundamental act by which we come to deeply understand who we are.
